persons.arguments_against: Strongest case against: there is a second Mainwaring biography in the corpus and 5 Mainwarings in the staff lists, so the stint could belong to a relative; also the staff list shows him as Assistant Police Magistrate through 1888 while the 1896 bio says 'asst. pol. mag., 1876-8' then 'pol. mag., 1888-91', which on its face conflicts with the 1878-1888 staff rows. This fails: (a) the staff-list man carries all three initials K. H. A. and the rank 'Captain' in every row, matching a retired R.N. captain — a same-surname relative would need the same three initials and the same naval rank; (b) the office sequence matches the biography on three distinct, specific posts: 'Stipendiary Magistrate' (bio: 'stipendiary magistrate, Jamaica, April, 1874'), 'Assistant Police Magistrate' (bio: 'asst. pol. mag.'), and 'Harbour Master' in 1889-1890 (bio: 'harbour-master, Kingston, 1876') — a coincidence across three offices in one colony is not realistic; (c) the apparent date conflict is a compression artifact of the 1896 bio (the fuller 1883 version says 'harbour-master, Kingston, Jamaica, and assistant police magistrate, Nov., 1876', i.e. he held both concurrently, which is exactly what the staff lists show resolving into separate rows over time). No counter-hypothesis survives the triple-office plus rank plus three-initial agreement.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second Captain K. H. A. Mainwaring in Jamaica in 1877-1890, or a record showing the staff-list magistrate was a different person than the harbour-master (e.g. distinct forenames behind the shared initials).

notes: The honour check fails only because neither side prints an honour. The bio's compressed 1896 dating ('asst. pol. mag., 1876-8') understates the tenure relative to the staff lists (asst. police magistrate through 1888), but the fuller 1883 bio version shows the harbour-master and assistant-police-magistrate posts were held concurrently from Nov. 1876, consistent with the staff-list rows.
